title: ReadFile logs interpret file names as Markdown in Agent chat window
author: imrehg
body: ### Reproduction steps 1. Start Zed in a project that has file names that can be interpreted as markdown (e.g. Python projects with `__init__.py` files.) 2. Start an Agent discussion, with a text that makes the agent read those files using the `ReadFile` tool, for example 3. See that the chat log has those filenames displayed as markdown rather than plain. ### Current vs. Expected behavior Current behaviour: Expected behaviour: The `__init__.py` files displayed that way, rather than the current __init__.py (`__` interpreted as bolding) ### Zed version and system specs Zed: v1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a (Zed) OS: macOS 26.5.2 Memory: 32 GiB Architecture: aarch64 ### Attach Zed log file N/A ### Relevant Zed settings N/A ### Relevant Keymap N/A ### (for AI issues) Model provider details Tried it with Claude Code (from Anthropic); also with Qwen Code with local model serving, both through ACP. This seems to be what Zed displays, rather than the models/harnesses. ### If you are using WSL on Windows, what flavor of Linux are you using? None

title: GPUI can not compile on pure wayland system
author: xhebox
body:
### Reproduction steps

When xkbcommon compiled with either wayland or x11 only, the current manifests will prevent it from compiling, despite gpui is already wayland/x11 seperated.


https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/blob/97110fd5a119eb5fad49524dc04d7c042193e8ab/crates/gpui_linux/Cargo.toml#L92

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Should only enable wayland features when it is wayland, vice versa.

### Zed version and system specs

97110fd5a119eb5fad49524dc04d7c042193e8ab

title: Focus-follows-mouse in Project panel is delayed if mouse isn't moving
author: injust
body:
### Reproduction steps

1. Enable focus-follows-mouse (`{ "focus_follows_mouse": { "enabled": true, "debounce_ms": 100 } }`)
2. Double-click a file in the Project panel to open it
3. Hover the mouse cursor over the Project panel and keep moving the mouse within the Project panel
4. Focus the buffer again
5. Do the same thing as 3), but stop moving the mouse as soon as it enters the Project panel

### Current vs. Expected behavior

The Project panel only gains focus properly if you keep moving the mouse cursor. If you stop moving the mouse cursor, there is a delay between the buffer losing focus and the Project panel gaining focus.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/47e88341-7db7-4c04-ba1f-f116c5a40d17

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a (Zed)
OS: macOS 15.7.7
Memory: 16 GiB
Architecture: x86_64

title: Focus-follows-mouse doesn't work on empty space in Project panel
author: injust
body:
### Reproduction steps

1. Enable focus-follows-mouse (`{ "focus_follows_mouse": { "enabled": true, "debounce_ms": 100 } }`)
2. Open a project with a short list of files, so there is empty space at the bottom of the Project panel
3. Open a buffer and focus it
4. Hover the mouse cursor over the empty space at the bottom of the Project panel

### Current vs. Expected behavior

The buffer stays focused. Instead, the Project panel should gain focus.

This is the same thing as https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/issues/39486, but with focus-follows-mouse instead of clicking.

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a (Zed) 
OS: macOS 15.7.7
Memory: 16 GiB
Architecture: x86_64

title: Billing system failures (and related UI failures) block development and waste AI tokens
author: brandonzylstra
body:
### Reproduction steps I was just telling someone recently how much nicer it was to use LLMs inside Zed rather than in Claude Code, and I wondered why I stopped working inside Zed. Now I remember one of the reasons: although the Zed editor is second-to-none, the billing system is another matter. Specifically in my case, my work was cut short with a message like this: I was shocked since I didn't think I could have burned through a month's worth of tokens in a couple of hours of moderate use, so I went to my account to see if I needed to add the ability to hit my credit card on-demand. The first problem is that the URL in the notice is not the place you go to do that. It's a first step, but it's not ultimately where you want to go. But that's nothing compared to the other problems. Adding money didn't have any effect, so I logged into my credit card site to make sure I had allocated funds for this particular charge. Yup, the max for Zed Industries was above the expected charge. Then I noticed something weird-I had never been billed for the initial sign up yesterday: After finding my way to the billing page and then the billing portal, I found the claim of an unpaid charge. If that were true, I would have seen a failed charge in my credit card web site. There was no record of any failed charge, however. There was only the record of a successful charge of $0. So the charge went through, but the charge that was attempted was ZERO. Through **no fault of my own**, Stripe didn't charge me, and then Zed suddenly stopped working in the middle of an AI session! ***This is the worst possible way to handle such a failure!*** Of course I have no idea where the fault is, whether it is entirely with Stripe, or some third-party system that connects your systems with theirs. But it adversely affects the ability to use Zed's paid AI service. So I re-attempted the charge, from the billing portal, and it immediately went through. "Now" I thought "I'll be able to resume my work". No such luck. No matter that I had paid (again!) for the account, Zed would not allow me to use it. I hoped that I was still within the 5-minute cache TTL that supposedly applies when using Anthropic models through Zed, but of course there was no way to know because nothing is made visible in the UI. So I tried to continue, and HOPED that I wouldn't pay a massive token penalty for having had my cache get cold because of the delay. Once it was clear that I could not possibly be within the 5 minute window, I gave up. The token hit from a cold cache can be MASSIVE. (Earlier today, outside of Zed, I saw it burn through 43% of a five-hour Anthropic session usage window with just ONE prompt.) The irony here is that the free features of Zed are (with maybe one exception) absolutely the best of the best. But the paid service (which most companies protect much more than their free services) is apparently being neglected. Here are the measures that I think would fix the problems: 1. Find out why billing failed and fix that. 2. Notify users of a billing problem WITHIN THE ZED UI ***LONG*** before their tokens are abruptly cut off! 3. Display available tokens/usage prominently in the Zed UI, so we know what to expect in general. 4. Display cache TTL (as far as I know this is always either 5 minutes or 1 hour with Anthropic models) **in the Zed UI***. ### Current vs. Expected behavior Current behavior is described above. Expected behavior: ***in the very least*** Zed's agent UI should start working again once Stripe has been kicked and made to reattempt its failed billing. But there are many other things that would have made this a better experience. ### Zed version and system specs Zed 1.10.2 Commit: adc60ccf12e199b8828bad3abb2591e147034734 Version: 1.10.2+stable.322.adc60ccf12e199b8828bad3abb2591e147034734 ### Attach Zed log file
Zed.log ```log ```
### Relevant Zed settings
settings.json ```json ```
### Relevant Keymap
keymap.json ```json ```
### (for AI issues) Model provider details Claude Opus 4.8 (through Zed AI) ### If you are using WSL on Windows, what flavor of Linux are you using? _No response_

title: Unable to connect to GitHub Copilot with npm install-strategy = nested
author: ardnew
body:
(This was originally posted as [a comment in #57764](https://github.com/zed-industries/zed/issues/57764#issuecomment-4953790054) but not fixed there. A fix needs to be tracked separately.) I was still unable to connect Copilot, with the same recurring error message described in #56877. The suggested workaround to use a predefined `GH_COPILOT_TOKEN` resulted with no change in behavior. The failure stemmed from my npm user configuration `~/.npmrc`, which contained: ``` install-strategy=nested ``` Changing this **_and_** reinstalling Zed fixed the issue: ``` $ npm config delete install-strategy # or set in ~/.npmrc: "install-strategy=hoisted" # or override via env: "export NPM_CONFIG_INSTALL_STRATEGY=hoisted" $ zed --uninstall $ curl -f https://zed.dev/install.sh | sh ``` Log files: - Zed

title: Semantic Tokenization: Distinguish Parameters from Local Variables
author: wrenger
body:
### Semantic Tokenization is Cool but Underutilized Zed currently does not fully utilize the semantic tokenization information #7450, #9461. The most prominent case is the usage of local variables and parameters. Even though their declarations can be highlighted differently, the usage is then indistinguishable. ### Example: At the declaration, the `parameter` is highlighted red, and the `local` variable is highlighted white. Later, both are highlighted white. So the question is: Why does the color of `parameter` change? Intuitively, it should remain red, as it is the same semantic item. In the highlight view, we also see that the semantic token is available and correctly classifying `parameter` as, in fact, a parameter in both cases. Zed, although, decides in the second case to interpret the parameter usage as "variable". From my perspective, this might as well be a bug or oversight. > This screenshot was made with the theme `Catppuccin Mocha`, but issue is the same with all other themes. Even though most do not highlight parameters differently in any case. ### Why does this matter? Keeping the same color for both the declaration and the usage is important to keep a visual link and avoid confusion. Moreover, having different colors for different "origins" of variables is also quite helpful. This is usually achieved with different naming conventions (and colors). Still, especially for more specific cases, like local variables and parameters, usually the same naming scheme is utilized. This makes accurate, distinguishable highlighting even more important. ### Possible approach We have all the information needed; just classifying parameters as parameters in all cases might suffice. Still, I might overlook something. ### Zed version and system specs Zed: v1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a (Zed) OS: macOS 26.5.2 Memory: 32 GiB Architecture: aarch64 ### Relevant Zed settings
settings.json ```json { "semantic_tokens": "combined" } ```

title: Remote (WSL/SSH): language server silently never starts after connection drop + reconnect (extension-load race)
author: interkelstar
body:
### Summary

After the Zed client's connection to a WSL/remote `zed-remote-server` drops mid-session and reconnects, language servers for already-open (or newly-opened) buffers sometimes **never start** — silently, with no error in the log. The workaround is manually running `editor: restart language server` (or "restart all language servers"), which works because by then extension loading has finished.

### Root cause (from log analysis)

1. The connection between the Zed client and the WSL remote-server intermittently drops:
   ```
   {"level":2,...,"message":"(remote server) stdin read failed: unexpected end of file"}
   {"level":2,...,"message":"(remote server) error reading message on stdin, dropping connection."}
   ...
   {"level":2,...,"message":"(remote server) timed out waiting for new connections after 600s. exiting."}
   ```
   This happened **12 times in a single day** in my logs (`server-workspace-5.log`), and is present in every remote-server log I have going back to June — so it's a frequent, long-standing occurrence, not a one-off.

2. When the client reconnects, a **new** `zed-remote-server` process starts and reloads all extensions from scratch:
   ```
   {"message":"(remote server) Loaded language server: docker-language-server"}
   {"message":"(remote server) Loaded language server: dockerfile-language-server"}
   {"message":"(remote server) Loaded language server: vscode-html-language-server"}
   {"message":"(remote server) Loaded language server: kotlin-language-server"}
   {"message":"(remote server) Loaded language server: kotlin-lsp"}
   {"message":"(remote server) Loaded language server: jdtls"}
   ```
   Extension loading is not instantaneous — some extensions (in my case, Java and a Kotlin dev extension that launches an extra native proxy process) take noticeably longer than others to finish registering.

3. If a matching buffer (e.g. a `.kt` or `.java` file, whether restored from the previous session or freshly opened) is evaluated for a language server **before** that language's extension has finished loading, Zed appears to check once, find no server registered yet, and never retries. No error is logged — the log simply never shows a `starting language server process` line for that server, only the earlier `Loaded language server: X` line once loading eventually completes.

4. Manually running "restart all language servers" (or restarting the specific server) after extensions have settled works immediately, because by then the server *is* registered and the match succeeds.

### Steps to reproduce

Hard to reproduce deterministically since it depends on the connection dropping and the extension-loading race window, but the pattern is:
1. Work in a Zed remote (SSH/WSL) project with at least one non-trivial extension providing a language server (Java, Kotlin, etc.).
2. Let the remote connection drop and reconnect (or simply reload/restart Zed while a `.kt`/`.java` buffer is open/restored).
3. Sometimes the corresponding language server never starts for that buffer — no errors, just silence — until "restart all language servers" is run manually.

### Expected

Either:
- Zed should retry matching a buffer to a language server once the relevant extension finishes loading (instead of only checking once at buffer-open time), or
- The remote connection should be more resilient to the underlying dropout in the first place.

### Environment
- Zed remote-server: `stable-1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a`
- Client: Zed on Windows, connecting to a WSL2 (Ubuntu) remote-server over `wsl.localhost`
- Affected language servers observed: `jdtls` (Java extension), `kotlin-lsp` (Kotlin extension, in my case a dev-extension build)
- The `stdin read failed` disconnects happen regardless of which extensions are installed; the "silently no LSP start" symptom is what depends on hitting the loading race window

title: Documentation: remote-development WSL docs say projects: open in wsl but actual action is OpenFolderInWsl
author: Shiven0504
body:
### Reproduction steps

1. Open `docs/src/remote-development.md`
2. Scroll to the WSL section
3. Read the “Opening a local folder in WSL” instructions

### Current vs. Expected behavior

## Current behavior

The docs say:

> To open a local folder inside a WSL container, use the `projects: open in wsl` action

However, the actual action type in code is `zed_actions::wsl_actions::OpenFolderInWsl`, which strongly suggests the correct command label should be `projects: open folder in wsl`, not `projects: open in wsl`.

## Expected behavior

The WSL docs should use the actual command name/alias that users can invoke from the command palette, or document the alias clearly if `projects: open in wsl` is intended to be accepted.

## Files involved

- `docs/src/remote-development.md`
- `crates/zed_actions/src/lib.rs`
- `crates/recent_projects/src/recent_projects.rs`

## Notes

This appears to be a documentation mismatch rather than a runtime bug, but it is user-facing because it can cause confusion when users try to follow the WSL setup docs.

title: Closing a terminal session that was converted from an agent session deletes the associated worktree — data loss
author: Asianfleet
body:
### Reproduction steps

1. Start Zed on Windows, open the Thread Sidebar with a local folder loaded.
2. In the Agent Panel, create a new git worktree in the project.
3. After the worktree is created, observe a new session entry appears in the Thread Sidebar, belonging to the new worktree. It defaults to "zed agent" mode.
4. Switch this session from "zed agent" to **"terminal"** mode.
5. Wait for the terminal session to initialize.
6. Close the terminal session.
7. → The worktree created in step 2 is **silently deleted from disk**. All files in that worktree, including uncommitted changes, are gone.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Current behavior:
Closing a terminal session that was originally a zed-agent session silently deletes the entire associated git worktree from disk. This has already caused me to lose work in progress files.

Expected behavior:
Closing a terminal session — or any session — should never delete a worktree from disk without at least a confirmation dialog. Ideally, session lifecycle (open/close/archive) and worktree lifecycle should be decoupled. Only explicit user actions (e.g. worktree picker trash button) should trigger worktree deletion.

### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a (Zed) 
OS: Windows 10.0.26200
Memory: 63.9 GiB
Architecture: x86_64
GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3080 Laptop GPU || NVIDIA Corporation || 591.86 r590_00

### Attach Zed log file

The log file (Zed.log) does not contain a Removed empty managed worktree directory line (unlike #57155), suggesting this may be a different deletion code path.

author: afgomez

title: macOS: modifier-only keybindings (shift shift) trigger while another app's non-activating panel has keyboard focus
author: Damecek
body:
Modifier-only keybindings (e.g. `shift shift`) trigger in Zed on macOS even when keyboard focus is in another app's non-activating panel (Spotlight-style popups such as ChatGPT's "Ask ChatGPT", Raycast, etc.).

### Description

With a modifier-only binding in `keymap.json`:

```json
{
  "context": "Workspace",
  "bindings": {
    "shift shift": "file_finder::Toggle"
  }
}
```

When a non-activating panel from another app has keyboard focus, Zed remains the active application (its window resigned key — the caret stops blinking — but the app is not deactivated). macOS routes `keyDown` events to the focused panel, but still delivers `flagsChanged:` events to the active app, i.e. Zed. Regular key chords are unaffected; only modifier-only bindings fire.

Cause in code:

- `crates/gpui/src/platform/mac/window.rs` — the `flagsChanged:` handler (`handle_view_event`, `PlatformInput::ModifiersChanged` branch) forwards the event with no `isKeyWindow` check.
- `crates/gpui/src/window.rs`, `dispatch_key_event` — the modifier-only keystroke synthesis (`ModifiersChangedEvent` → synthetic `"shift"` keystroke) does not check `self.is_window_active()`, so the keymap matcher matches `shift shift` in an unfocused window.

A guard on the keystroke synthesis only (not on `ModifiersChanged` dispatch as a whole, which is also used for hover states like cmd-hover go-to-definition) would fix this.

Steps to reproduce:
1. Add `"shift shift": "file_finder::Toggle"` (context `Workspace`) to `keymap.json`.
2. Focus a Zed window.
3. Open a non-activating panel of another app (e.g. ChatGPT's "Ask ChatGPT" popup via its global shortcut) so it has keyboard focus.
4. Press Shift twice.

**Expected Behavior**: Nothing happens in Zed; the keystrokes belong to the focused panel.
**Actual Behavior**: Zed opens the file finder while the panel keeps keyboard focus.

### Zed Version and System Specs

Zed: v1.10.3 (Zed)
OS: macOS 26.5.1 (25F80)
Memory: 16 GiB
Architecture: aarch64

title: Markdown Preview: toggle preview in current tab instead of opening a new tab
author: yngfng
body:
## Summary

When opening the Markdown preview from a `.md` file, Zed currently opens the preview in a **new tab** (as a separate editor item). This breaks the mental model of "preview as another view of the same document."

## Current behavior

```
Markdown Editor  →  Open Preview  →  New Preview Tab (separate item)
```

- The preview opens as a distinct editor tab alongside or after the source file.
- Toggling the preview off closes the preview tab but leaves an empty tab artifact.
- The source and preview feel like two independent files rather than two faces of the same document.

## Proposed behavior

```
Markdown Editor  ⇄  Markdown Preview
```

The preview should behave as a **view toggle within the same tab**, similar to how VS Code toggles between source and rendered views. The tab remains the same; only the visible mode switches between editing and preview.

## Why this matters

- **Single-tab workflow**: users browsing multiple markdown files end up with tab explosion (every source file spawns a companion preview tab).
- **Consistency**: other editors (VS Code, Obsidian, Typora) treat preview as a mode switch, not a new document.
- **Navigation clarity**: closing a tab should close both source and preview, not leave orphans.

## Optional setting

To accommodate different preferences, consider exposing:

```jsonc
{
  "markdown.preview.open_mode": "replace" | "tab" | "split"
}
```

| Mode     | Behavior |
|----------|----------|
| `replace`| Preview replaces the current tab (the proposal above) |
| `tab`    | Open preview in a new tab (current behavior) |
| `split`  | Open preview in a split pane alongside the source |

The default could remain `"tab"` for backward compatibility, while `"replace"` would give users the single-tab workflow many expect.

## Related

- Discussion #42993 — request for auto-preview on file open
- PR #58752 — `preview_tabs.auto_preview` for md/svg/csv

This proposal is complementary: auto-preview controls *when* to open, while `open_mode` controls *where* it opens.

title: Duplicating a project panel entry overwrites the system clipboard
author: shindgew
body:
### Reproduction steps

1. Copy recognizable content to the system clipboard, such as the text `keep me`.
2. Open a project in Zed.
3. In the project panel, right-click a file and select **Duplicate**.
4. Paste the clipboard contents into any text field.

The issue also applies to folders because files and folders use the same Duplicate action.

### Current vs. Expected behavior

Current behavior:

The selected file or folder is duplicated successfully, but the previous system clipboard contents are replaced with the selected entry's path.

For example, after copying `keep me` and duplicating `example.txt`, pasting no longer produces `keep me`; it produces the path/name of `example.txt`.

Expected behavior:

Duplicate should create a copy of the selected project entry without modifying the system clipboard. The existing clipboard contents should remain available after duplication.

The Copy and Cut actions should continue writing file paths to the system clipboard as designed.

### Additional context

This is present on `main` at commit `aeeacf5439b2d30d01e38d65d767e6f31b255ecc`.

`ProjectPanel::duplicate` currently invokes the regular Copy action followed by Paste. Copy writes the selected entry paths to the system clipboard, so Duplicate inherits an unintended clipboard side effect.

This appears to be a regression from #49367, which added system-clipboard writes to project-panel Copy and Cut.

The related open PRs #60871 and #53825 concern cross-window file copy/paste, but neither currently changes the Duplicate action or addresses this behavior.


### Zed version and system specs

Zed: v1.11.1+preview.319.8a00e905d118362fc4a25b471d880c3108e50e75 (Zed Preview) 
OS: macOS 27.0 (Build 26A5378j)
Memory: 24 GiB
Architecture: aarch64

title: Git panel / project panel stale when files are modified by an agent running in a different Zed window/project
author: mingchiuli
body:
### Reproduction steps

### Summary
Git panel and project panel do not reflect file changes when the modification comes from an AI agent running in a *different* Zed window/project than the one whose repository is being modified.

### Description
When an agent (via Zed's Agent Panel, ACP external agent, or a terminal-based agent tool) writes directly to files belonging to a project/repository that is open in a *different* Zed window than the one the agent is attached to, the target window's Git panel and Project panel do not pick up the change. `git status` on the command line shows the correct diff, but Zed's UI stays stale until the window is manually reloaded/restarted.

This looks related to the file-watcher / stale-git-state class of issues previously tracked in #38109 and #49436 (both closed, with fixes landing in #59714, #57895, #59560). However this specific case — cross-window / cross-project agent writes — still reproduces on a version that includes those fixes.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Open Project A in one Zed window, Project B in a separate Zed window.
2. In Project A's window, run an agent (Agent Panel or ACP external agent) and have it modify a tracked file that belongs to Project B's repository (e.g. by pointing it at Project B's path).
3. Switch focus to Project B's Zed window.
4. Observe the Git panel and Project panel in Project B's window.
5. Run `git status` in a terminal for Project B — it shows the actual changes.





### Current vs. Expected behavior

### Current behavior
- Git panel in Project B's window shows no changes (or stale changes).
- Project panel does not reflect the modified/added files.
- Requires manually reloading the window (or making an additional edit) to force a refresh.

### Expected behavior
- Git panel and Project panel should reflect the true on-disk/git state regardless of which process (including an agent running in another Zed window) made the change, consistent with how Zed already handles external terminal-driven changes within the same window's project.

### Zed version and system specs

### Zed Version and System Specs
Zed 1.10.3 
Commit: 0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a
Version: 1.10.3+stable.324.0c54c414d522234de7298039708ffe85a116892a
OS: Mac OS 26.5.2 (25F84)

### Additional context
- Related closed issues: #38109, #49436
- Related fixes that may be adjacent but don't cover this case: #59714, #57895, #59560
- Related agent+git issues: #52427, #55869

title: Zed agent starts MCP under root (/)
author: fominok
body:
### Reproduction steps

Hi, I’m trying to use my MCP server with Zed’s built-in Agent. I also tested the same MCP server with OpenCode running inside Zed for comparison.

OpenCode correctly uses the current project directory as its working directory. Zed Agent, however, appears to run the MCP server from `/`.

As a result, cache files and other generated files that should be created inside the current project directory are instead written to the filesystem root and unsurprisingly that fails

### Current vs. Expected behavior

MCP status report called from an agent:

Current (Zed's built-in agent): 
`Watch dir | /`

Expected (as in OpenCode via Zed right now):
`Watch dir | /Users/user/Documents/projects/backend/mr`
